title: AI for All except weapons
summary: Welcomes the domestic focus but argues the strategy would be stronger with guardrails on AI use in defence, security, and weapons.
debate_point: A public-interest AI strategy may need an explicit military and autonomous weapons boundary, even when the launch focuses on civilian uses.

title: AI for All, details to follow
summary: Argues the strategy is ambitious and has useful choices, but defers too many hard policy questions around transparency, privacy, automated decision-making, and enforceable AI rules.
debate_point: Trust cannot come only from spending, privacy reform, and voluntary certification; Canada may need clearer transparency obligations.

title: Canada's AI for All strategy: skeptical guide
summary: Treats the launch as a large economic, workforce, sovereignty, and trust package while tracking where the public should keep asking for receipts.
debate_point: The strategy is not only a policy promise; it is an ongoing accountability surface for labour, energy, procurement, literacy, and governance.

title: Canada's AI strategy promises to protect citizens. Critics say it still lacks teeth
summary: Collects criticism that the protection side of the strategy is still light on concrete child safety, online harms, privacy, and enforcement measures.
debate_point: The strategy promises protection, but critics want more specific legal duties and remedies.

title: Canada's AI strategy: regulatory direction and SMB support
summary: A pre-launch LinkedIn discussion asks for clearer regulatory direction and faster, meaningful support for Canadian SMBs and responsible ecosystem-building.
debate_point: Small organizations need both adoption support and clarity on responsible use before the strategy becomes practical.

title: Carney warns foreign AI platforms can be used against Canadians
summary: Captures the strategy's core sovereignty argument: dependence on foreign platforms creates data, security, economic, and values risks.
debate_point: Canada is betting that sovereign compute and trusted alliances can reduce dependence on hyperscalers without isolating the country.

title: CCI statement on the federal government's AI strategy
summary: Welcomes the strategy's focus on Canadian innovators while arguing implementation should be judged by whether Canadian firms scale globally and capture more value.
debate_point: The pro-strategy case is that Canadian prosperity depends on building firms, IP, and policy capacity at home.

title: Five things missing from Canada's AI strategy
summary: Frames public questions around missing timelines, unclear job impacts, data centre implications, and how promises will be delivered.
debate_point: The launch creates targets, but readers still need timelines, delivery mechanics, labour impact estimates, and infrastructure tradeoffs.

title: Understanding Canada's National Artificial Intelligence Strategy
summary: Highlights implementation questions around trust, funding access, policy coherence, delivery partners, and the capital-intensive sovereign compute buildout.
debate_point: The strategy's success depends less on the launch document and more on coherent delivery across departments, partners, provinces, and budgets.
